name: Ines Burrell
shortBio: Geopolitical analyst and advisor — Russia, Eastern Europe, Eurasia. Former journalist; published in Persuasion; live broadcast commentator.

description: Bespoke geopolitical advisory and political risk consulting. Russia, Eastern Europe and Eurasia expertise for businesses and organisations navigating global uncertainty.

title: A Short Course in Russian Diplomacy
excerpt: Demand, threaten, receive, and then do it anyway. Russia has produced a closed-loop demonstration of what its diplomacy actually is.

heroImage.alt: A Short Course in Russian Diplomacy

children.text: The killing of 24 people by Russia in a Kyiv apartment complex, along with other civilians elsewhere in Ukraine, following the 9 May 'ceasefire', has been presented by many commentators as Putin's revenge for the humiliation he suffered at the hands of the Ukrainian president, Volodymyr Zelenskyy, and the permission to hold the military parade Zelenskyy granted.

This argument for some reason assumes that Russia would not have targeted a block of flats unless severely insulted. As if executing random civilians, although objectionable in itself, is nevertheless a reasonable course of action under specific circumstances. It also ignores the several hundred similar attacks Russia has perpetrated over the past four years, which the international community has accepted as a given.

What most of the commentators have missed though is that Russia has created a closed-loop demonstration of what happens when it wants to get something from the adversary and how its behaviour changes once it has got it. In essence, this is a perfect specimen of Russia's approach to diplomatic processes: they demand something, threaten violence should they not get it, receive what they demanded in exchange for not perpetrating that violence, and then commit it anyway.

Although for many people familiar with Russia's behaviour this is not new, the significance of the latest performance stems from the fact that the Kremlin managed to create a complete, observable and irrefutable argument short enough to be presented to the most ardent followers of the 'we can negotiate peace with Russia' camp.

Members of this camp have managed to ignore the late lamented Budapest Memorandum. They have disregarded that Russia, a permanent member of the UN Security Council, breaks the UN Charter daily. They have set aside the fact that Russia has violated every Geneva Convention — in other words, the entire corpus of international law governing the coexistence of nations. In their view, the war in Ukraine can be negotiated to an end because this time Russia would not break the agreement.

All the Russian rule-breaking has happened over a long period or has been muddied by disinformation campaigns implanting fake facts into real stories. As a result, even those who were present at the events are often not believed by those determined not to believe them.

This time is different. Russia demanded a ceasefire for 9 May, threatened Kyiv with annihilation should Ukraine not comply, enjoyed the ceasefire Donald Trump brokered, and then attacked Kyiv with everything it had once the ceasefire was over. All in the space of a few short days, and not just in full view of the entire world but, most importantly, under the watchful eye of both President Zelenskyy and the Ukrainian nation. Russia has created a perfect demonstration of cause and effect, an 'if – then' exercise: if Ukraine negotiates with Russia and fulfils its part of the agreement, then Russia will take what it was given and attack anyway.

Ukraine has had to publicly go along with the idea that peace can be magically achieved if only Russia can be persuaded to be happy. The mere existence of this sequence releases Kyiv from the obligation to pretend that peace with Russia can be negotiated as long as the Ukrainian side tries hard enough. This is also the point where any supporters of non-escalation lose the fig-leaf they have employed to ignore the argument, made by anybody who has ever been on the receiving end of Russian aggression, that Russia cannot be negotiated with.

Russia has effectively spent the last agreement it was ever going to get from Ukraine. After this, there will be no popular support for any agreement with Russia unless Russia can be forced to comply, and even then the force would have to be exercised only by Ukraine, nobody else. The only agreements Ukraine will ever sign with Russia are the ones it signs with a proverbial gun to Russia's head.

title: At Least the Pope Is Fine This Time
excerpt: Russia has now poured money into three European elections and lost all three. What Hungary's result says about the fortunes of Europe's far right.

heroImage.alt: At Least the Pope Is Fine This Time

children.text: Now that the political event of the year - Hungarian elections are over to the overall satisfaction of non-Russia aligned Europeans, we can be absolutely sure of two things.

First, this is the third election that Russia has poured its soul and money into… and lost spectacularly. According to various reports, Russia invested approximately €200 million into vote buying and other subversion operations in elections in Moldova (which according to the Moldovan PM represented 1% of Moldovan GDP). That did not pan out. And then, of course, there was their attempt to install a completely unknown candidate as president in Romania (Calin Georgescu, a fringe ultranationalist who had polled in single digits until days before the vote) which was thwarted by cancelling the result and redoing the entire election from scratch.

And now this. Russia is definitely losing its touch. I would say that perhaps Europe is getting smarter, but Slovakia did elect Fico, and Czechia did elect Babiš. They, however, are a different story – these parts of Eastern Europe are still full of older generation voters who remember cheap rents and forget the empty supermarket shelves and Russian tanks. This too shall pass.

And second - the US Vice President, JD Vance, has all the appeal of a wet squid. When he insulted Europeans at the Munich Security Conference in 2025, the attraction of his eyeliner-traced eyes shrank considerably. Now, he cost Viktor Orbán some of his already limited votes. Perhaps we can rent him next time there is a difficult political decision to make in Europe?

Vance's boss, Donald Trump, had a similar effect on events. The Canadian Conservative Party was set to win its April 2025 election by a comfortable margin – until Trump inserted himself into the situation. Pierre Poilievre, the Conservative leader, attempted to distance himself from MAGA, but the damage was done by simple association.
So what can the elections in Hungary tell us about the general state of affairs of the right-wing parties in the Western world and their potential fortunes?

Hungarian elections were widely treated as a test case showing what Trump will do when his time to vacate the White House comes. If Orbán walked away without putting up a fight, the consensus was that Trump might follow suit. Considering Orbán has conceded defeat, Trump would have to set that precedent all alone.

As to matters on the European continent, the next elections where the far right can take over power and where the US dream team can be 'helpful' are in France, Italy, and Poland in 2027. Considering that the Trump and MAGA effect has brought the opposite effect, the right-wing actors in Europe, at least those with electoral ambitions beyond their own base, should make obvious conclusions and avoid them like the plague.

They might also go for a less pro-Russian and more straightforward conservative approach. Marine Le Pen's Rassemblement National (and her replacement Jordan Bardella, since she cannot run for the post of President of France as a result of a fund embezzlement conviction) has considerably toned down its pro-Russia leanings and is starting to look almost sane. Italy's Giorgia Meloni and Fratelli d'Italia, who are up for re-election in 2027, have been one of the most stable supporters of European defence and unity. Meloni, whatever her origins, has turned out to be something the European right rarely produces — a reliable ally.

All in all, it seems that despite the entirely understandable animosity Europeans feel toward the Washington duo, we should be, in an odd way, rather grateful to them. By making themselves so unlikable and anti-European, they inadvertently might have cured at least some of Europe of their particular brand of populism without Europe having to go through the disease itself. Or so we can hope.

title: Don't Watch the Russian Economy. Watch the Repressions.
excerpt: Economic collapse in Russia will not arrive as protest or regime fracture. In a totalitarian state, the real signal of systemic fracture is escalating repression.

heroImage.alt: Don't Watch the Russian Economy. Watch the Repressions.

children.text: The state of the Russian economy has been discussed in the news from all sides. There are predictions of both its imminent demise and relative immortality. There are calculations on how the Strait of Hormuz oil crisis affects it, and just recently there was an excellent report from Swedish military intelligence on artificial inflation of Russian economic prowess.

All of these view the collapse of the Russian economy as the final desirable outcome, and even Russian economists in exile are not rooting for Russian economic deliverance. The consensus is that it is going to collapse eventually, and nobody really knows when.
But What Do We Expect Such Collapse to Achieve?

It is generally accepted that wars end when parties change their goals or have exhausted their physical, military, and economic potential for continuing them. While waiting for Russia to change its mind on wanting to conquer neighbouring states might take a few lifetimes, the economic collapse is quite reasonably expected to serve as an objective limiting factor in its expansionist agenda. When people are starving, even hardened regimes have to turn inwards. By mapping the impending economic doom, we should be able to anticipate how long Russia can continue the war in Ukraine and plan our own actions.

However, these arguments discount the real variable in the equation — the Russian people. There is not much point in commenting on the health (or lack thereof) of the Russian economy if its people react in a completely different way than they would in countries where human life is a value.

And because of this variable, even when the Russian economy finally collapses, we may not notice — because collapse in Russia is unlikely to produce the visible markers the West is trained to read. It will not arrive as mass protest or regime fracture. It will arrive as degradation, absorbed by society and redirected into the war effort.

In a word, we are watching for the wrong metric. In a country where the pain threshold is close to that experienced in Europe in the Middle Ages, economic data on paper does not translate into results as expected.
Train Wreck Already in Progress

Generally, an economic collapse is marked by currency collapse, bank runs, default, hyperinflation, mass unemployment, and societal unrest. Some of these have already happened, and some are well on the way there.

The rouble has been maintained artificially since 2022. The Russian Central Bank effectively sacrificed it and the Russian economy at the start of the war by taking steps that were meant to retain the appearance of stability, instead of actually stabilising the situation. As most investment was withdrawn from the Russian economy (and no, China does not bring in investment, it extracts resources — even in Russia), Russia had to battle the mixture of cash shortage and post-Covid inflation with the only means possible — high interest rates. This has had a negative effect on the already battered economy and caused a loop where economic medicine effectively kills the patient — the economy. Caught between the inflation caused by printing money and the business collapse caused by high interest rates, the Central Bank is simultaneously administering both remedies, each of which makes the other necessary. The war budget cannot be cut, oil revenues are falling, and the deficit is being financed by money printing that the high interest rates cannot fully offset — the balance is holding only because it has not yet broken.

Some version of the signals of collapse have already happened. The currency is maintained artificially. The ordinary population attempted to withdraw their foreign currency deposits in 2022 and was blocked, capped at $10,000 in cash, with any remaining balance only accessible in roubles at the Central Bank's rate, restrictions that remain in force today. There is a form of bank run happening at the moment in response to the blocking of internet services — if the only way to pay for services is cash, since the card machines do not work, then keeping money in the bank is useless. This is evidenced by $19.7 billion withdrawn in cash in January alone. Technical default has already happened in June 2022. Each of these would register as a crisis signal anywhere else. In Russia, they are absorbed as the new normal.

Curiously enough, mass unemployment is happening alongside mass worker shortages — there are areas, especially rural ones, where no work can be found, and areas where companies cannot find enough workers. Mobilisation and the wartime defence industry have stripped labour from civilian sectors, creating acute shortages in some regions while the collapse of import-dependent manufacturing and retail has left others with nothing to employ people in.
Unrest That Is Not Happening
The only element we have not seen and it seems will not see is societal unrest, which should be the culmination of descent through the ranks and the sign of changes to come.

Unlike the situation in Iran, where economic hardship can potentially cause the change of regime because people there are unhappy and have agency, people in Russia are usually unhappy but they have no agency. Russians historically have high tolerance for pain and abuse, and the current generation is not very different. They are used to decisions being made for them, not by them.

These are people who can be press-ganged into military, can be tortured by their commanders, abused, and then sent on suicide missions on the front, and instead of turning their weapons against their torturers, they follow the orders and die.

These are also the same people who already experienced the collapse of the economy in the 90s following the disintegration of the USSR. The common response was to organise a criminal gang and survive by robbing others. They are now effectively doing the same — killing and robbing their neighbours in hopes that it will serve as a reprieve for them.
Total Pressure on Totalitarian State

Russia has almost completed the transition from authoritarian state to totalitarian, achieving a form of control the Soviet Union itself did not fully reach, one that extends into private life, private relationships, and private thought. The totality of the government's control is very close to what it used to be in the Soviet Union. The only two differences — there is some semblance of private property in modern Russia, and people are allowed to leave. All other aspects of human life come under complete control of the state. In the absence of any grassroots movements and the complete absence of any horizontal societal bonds, these people will learn to live in the conditions of a collapsed economy just as they have learned to live with sending their loved ones to die in another country in exchange for generous compensation packages.

However, this is not an argument against applying economic pressure on Russia. Quite the opposite. Extreme sanctions are needed to elicit even a modicum of reaction from the people who are used to very brutish life, as well as elites, who have no power but will follow anybody who has finally had enough. The security apparatus is the last to notice and the last to care — repression offers its own rewards that a salary cannot fully explain.

Just recently, Gennady Zyuganov, leader of Russia's Communist Party and a derivative of the system, warned the Russian State Duma that without urgent action to improve the economy 'a repeat of what happened in 1917 awaits us.' Apart from the irony of a communist warning about the imminent approach of a communist 'revolution', this serves as the indication that the system, far from collapsing, is still allowing its loyal fixtures to let off steam. Albeit with consent.

The metric worth watching is not economic. In a totalitarian state, the real signal of systemic fracture is escalating repression. A stable regime does not need to resort to additional punishment to stave off dissent, it only does so when the existing level of control is no longer sufficient. And when the system runs out of real enemies and starts consuming its own (loyalists, collaborators, true believers – Zyuganovs, in other words), we can expect change. Watch for that, not GDP.

title: Has Reality Stopped Obeying Putin?
excerpt: Putin has suddenly become extremely active. This time it is not only his adversaries noticing that reality and his version of events live in different universes.

children.text: This past week has seen a flurry of activity from Vladimir Putin. On 28 June, he spoke to the United Russia party congress, to the heads of Russia's largest oil companies, and appeared in an interview with state TV propagandist Pavel Zarubin while visibly reading from a teleprompter. On 3 July, he participated in the recording of a staged visit to what was presented as a military command post. And on 4 July, he spoke to Donald Trump on the phone, which was publicised by Putin's foreign policy aide Yuri Ushakov.

All of this is happening against the backdrop of a severe fuel crisis and the unravelling economic catastrophe the likes of which Russia has not seen for decades. For somebody who has a habit of disappearing from public life at the first sign of any trouble (as he did when the submarine Kursk went down), Putin has suddenly become extremely active. This does look like a bog-standard damage limitation campaign; however, this time it is obviously not working. Because this time it is not only his adversaries who are noticing that the reality and Putin's version of events are not just diverging but essentially located in completely different universes. This time the mismatch is visible across his entire kingdom.
The Disappearing Act

The first serious signs of the impending crisis were in early June, when Ukraine played with Russia like cat and mouse, hitting targets in St Petersburg on 3 and 4 June, on the eve of Putin's appearance at the St Petersburg International Economic Forum. His appearance there on 5 June, during which he completely ignored the mayhem Ukraine had caused in his native city, was his last public appearance for almost three weeks. He did not react to attacks on oil refineries and storage tanks, nor to the burning 15th Naval Arsenal near St Petersburg. There was no comment from him after the largest drone attack on Moscow of the entire full-scale war on 18 June. By the time he felt capable of facing the public on 28 June, only two of Russia's ten largest refineries were left untouched, and only because both of them were beyond the Urals.
In the meantime, even before the fuel crisis, discontent had been brewing on many levels.

In mid-March, Ilya Remeslo, a pro-Kremlin blogger and lawyer, posted a call for Putin to face trial and labelled the war a "dead end". He was taken to a psychiatric hospital in St Petersburg the following day but was later released and is still posting what would normally amount to "discrediting the armed forces" and would earn anybody else a one-way ticket to a Russian penal colony.

Victoria Bonya, a Russian beauty influencer living in Monaco with 13 million Instagram followers, posted a video in April addressed directly to Putin, complaining about catastrophic flooding, internet blackouts, livestock culls and economic pressure. The Kremlin uncharacteristically acknowledged the video, and the story died down.

While Bonya was contained and Remeslo, despite piping up from time to time, was seen as more of a product of mental illness due to his stint in a psychiatric hospital, what both of these individuals did and what the Kremlin could not stop was that they put a name to the problem. Both named Vladimir Putin as the person responsible. And both are still alive and unmolested.
The Floodgates
This seemed to open the floodgates. Suddenly, it was fine to admit that the budget deficit, unrelenting inflation and sky-high interest rates were the result of the war that would not end.

The massive losses of military personnel, coupled with slowing or even reversing advances of the Russian army and regressive management methods in the army, were dragged into daylight when a Russian combat veteran named Alexander Lunin posted a video on 25 June demanding a live meeting with Putin, threatening that the army would "turn its weapons against the Kremlin" if ignored. This happened almost exactly three years after Prigozhin's mutiny. The video reached 12 million views within 24 hours. Lunin was arrested two days later, but he is no longer an anomaly.

All this, coupled with internet blackouts orchestrated by the FSB and now fuel supply collapse, created a situation where multiple problems all had one culprit: the Kremlin, aka Putin. Public figures from within the system were no longer staying silent – German Gref, the head of Sberbank, Russia's largest bank, said on state television that he did not know a single person "who isn't concerned about anything other than a rapid end of hostilities."

And if that was not enough, the fuel shortages created the very situation the Kremlin had been trying to avoid since the start of 2022. In a country where any public meetings and manifestations are still banned, citing reasons ranging from tree inspections to, yes, still-active COVID restrictions, many people are now congregating in the same spots looking for fuel. And although for now they seem to be more interested in fighting each other for any fuel scraps thrown their way, eventually their collective mood might swing in the direction of those responsible for the debacle.
Matching Reality

While the overall consensus is that the USSR collapsed mostly due to catastrophic economic failures, one of the major contributing factors most people disregard was the stark disparity between reality and the public message from the ruling class. Empty shelves at the shops and claims of economic superiority over the West did not land the desired message.

This is why in a repressive state like Russia the message the state sends to its subjects cannot diverge sharply from what these subjects can observe with their own eyes. After local governments took the "fuel shortage was created by panic buying" line as far as they could, the Kremlin had to acknowledge the problem to some extent. However, Putin's plan, according to the teleprompter interview, was not to stop the war. Quite the opposite: he assured everybody that Russia would continue fighting until all goals were met, not that anybody knows what those goals are.

As a solution, Putin demanded increased production of air defence systems and fuel, and later, at what was presented as a military command post on 3 July, he ordered the creation of a buffer zone in Ukraine as a way to decrease attacks on Russia. As if ordering increased production of fuel and the creation of a buffer zone could magic them into existence and stop Ukrainian attacks. Putin has been ordering the creation of a buffer zone for a few years now, and the buffer zone has not complied.

It is becoming clear that Russia cannot stop Ukraine from attacking its oil infrastructure and military targets. While the size of the Russian Federation is a particular source of pride for Russians, this is where they might have wished they had not absorbed quite so many neighbours. Ukraine, well over twenty times smaller than Russia, has a job maintaining adequate air defence, and it has all of NATO to help it. Russia is virtually unprotectable in this sense. The situation is going to get worse.

Traditionally, when autocrats face domestic problems, they tend to look for an external outlet to let off steam. When Russia seized Crimea in 2014, it was an exercise intended to increase Putin's popularity at home, as was the case with the Russo-Japanese War of 1904, launched partly to shore up the Tsar's flagging domestic standing.

So it is no wonder that in his speech on 3 July, Putin tasked his general staff with determining which countries were preventing Ukraine from capitulating and coming up with an appropriate course of action.
Deflecting the Problem

In the light of this, the US warning to Poland, which became public on 3 July, about a possible Russian armed provocation designed to test NATO's resolve and pressure Kyiv's partners into suspending aid to Ukraine, as well as the Latvian intelligence service's warning of 23 June that Russia is preparing hybrid attacks against the Baltic states or Poland including drone and missile strikes, should not be taken lightly. Russia is also doubling down on its claim that the Baltic states have opened their airspace for Ukrainian drone strikes on Russia, which functions as a ready-made pretext for retaliation. The domestic pressure Putin is facing is growing. He needs to show he is still a predator, especially to his own people. As he did before, and as many have done before him, Putin might deflect the problem by moving it elsewhere, even if just for a while.

As long as he believes NATO will not respond, or that the response will not be painful enough to affect him personally, he might deploy a limited test or two against one of the neighbouring states, although it is highly unlikely to be a direct military aggression at this time.
However, none of this means the end is near.

title: Have Markets Decided to Ride Out the Oil Crisis of 2026?
excerpt: Spot prices follow the tweets; futures hold a pre-determined course. What oil markets are quietly telling us about the direction of the US-Iran war.

heroImage.alt: Have Markets Decided to Ride Out the Oil Crisis of 2026?

children.text: The reason many people follow the US-Iran war events is the uncertainty around oil prices and their effect on the global economy and by extension the household bills. We want to know how long this is going to last, mostly to figure out how bad it is going to get. Following the Washington administration's logic is no easy task, however, oil markets seem to have made up their mind about what lies ahead. What do current oil prices tell us about the direction of the conflict and the economic pain it will inflict globally?
Tactical and strategic
First things first. There are two types of oil prices – futures and spot prices.

As the name indicates, futures relate to deals for the sale of barrels of oil in the future. Generally, a few months down the line. The oil sold this way usually has not been loaded on a ship yet; what's more, it might still be in the ground. The energy market factors in the difficulties associated with extraction, loading, and transportation. Essentially, this is the price the market has determined oil will be worth by the time it is ready for delivery, accounting for everything that might affect supply between now and then.

Spot prices, on the other hand, apply to oil not only extracted, but already loaded on a ship, and in some cases even delivered. It is the price the buyer pays for the specific boatload of oil on the spot – hence the name, as "spot" means immediate, on-the-spot delivery. Oil sold this way can sell for a lot more or a lot less than futures because its price is affected by the global and economic events of the day. On days when there is plenty of oil on the market, an additional discount might be needed to offload it, and during shortage periods any additional geopolitical events can send it sky high.

In simple terms, futures define the long-term, strategic outlook on the overall state of the global economy as it pertains to oil. Spot prices reflect only current events, without taking into account how they will develop past a certain point in time and are mostly an opportunistic indicator.
The bigger the crisis, the calmer the market?
Since comprehensive oil price records began in 1988, the world has seen three most comparable supply-shock crises – in 2008, 2022, and now in 2026.

At the height of the 2008 crisis, futures prices peaked at $146.08, and spot prices tracked almost identically, reaching $143.95. This crisis was not caused by a supply disruption. It was demand-driven and speculation-amplified as a result of the swift growth of the Chinese economy.

The 2022 crisis was closer in nature to the current one. It was based on a shortage of oil, albeit a virtual shortage – the oil had not left the market. However, markets reacted in anticipation to what would happen if that oil was removed from the market. Futures prices reached $127.98, while the spot prices went slightly higher – $133.18.

The current crisis so far has also been caused by a virtual shortage of oil. It is worth noting that an oil shortage only starts from the moment deliveries no longer arrive at their destination, not from the moment they no longer ship — meaning we have not yet experienced the full physical impact of the Hormuz closure. Futures prices peaked at $119.50. Spot prices, unlike before, have jumped far higher, reaching $138.21.
What does that tell us?
Speculative trade in 2008 created more pain for consumers than real physical shortages that are already materialising in 2026.

Despite the possibility of a real oil deficit, the markets are less worried about its impact than they were in 2022 when the withdrawal of roughly 12% of globally traded crude oil (Russia's share at the time) was contemplated, compared to the 20% of globally traded crude oil currently locked up in the Persian Gulf.

What is remarkable though, is that while spot prices are yo-yoing and seemingly following Donald Trump's tweets rather closely, futures markets appear to be ignoring them entirely and holding what looks like a pre-determined course. They hover steadily around a $105 median.

This tells us that the markets are expecting to ride out the current crisis and do not currently expect disruption beyond what has already occurred. There are various reasons for this – they might genuinely believe that the US and Iran have already done their worst; they can bank on the ability of free market economies to adapt to most circumstances, or they might remember that mid-term elections in the US are coming up, and Donald Trump should (one might think) want to put this conflict to rest by then.

Therefore, if we are to believe the oil markets, they expect more of the same for now, and most probably no serious change until closer to the US mid-term elections. Which is probably more stability (albeit at a higher price than we would like) than we have seen these last couple of months.

title: Public Humiliation Can Be Fatal for Aging Dictators
excerpt: Zelenskyy authorised Russia's parade, and the Kremlin had to accept. In a system where authority derives from the appearance of strength, this bell cannot be unrung.

heroImage.alt: Public Humiliation Can Be Fatal for Aging Dictators

children.text: When Ukraine's president, Volodymyr Zelenskyy, issued a decree on 8 May authorising Russia to hold the military parade on Red Square on 9 May from 10am Kyiv time, many took it as a brilliant publicity stunt and masterful trolling. In reality, Zelenskyy was dead serious. He did authorise the parade. From this year onwards until the end of the war, come May, the Kremlin will need to seek Kyiv's permission to hold the parade in front of the mausoleum holding Lenin's corpse, because the expectation will be that Kyiv will otherwise gatecrash it. Putin knows it. Putin's elites know it. This bell cannot be unrung.
Pathetic Spectacle by the Mausoleum

In hindsight, it might have been more prudent for Putin to chance the Ukrainian long-range drones and Flamingo missiles. It is highly likely that Kyiv would not have struck Moscow anyhow — the countless rings of air defence around Moscow left other targets wide open, and Ukraine might have considered those juicier. Putin could have framed any attacks as proof of righteous war. It would have been a weak response, but he might have looked more like a man and less like Gollum during his brief speech at the parade. Instead, he decided to believe Ukraine would crash his party, and paid for it by looking pitiful.

A popular joke in Russia suggested that Russians should persuade Zelenskyy to authorise the return of mobile internet, blocked on and off for the past few months. Another joke spoke of popular surprise at seeing a president who allows things instead of banning them.

In a system where authority is derived entirely from the appearance of strength, any sign of weakness is treated as a cause to question that authority. This is especially true in Russia. Russians accept ruthless leaders, they do not accept weak ones.

If this was not enough, earlier Moscow had tried to threaten Kyiv with the usual end of the world to get its way on the parade front. The Kremlin declared a unilateral ceasefire from midnight on 8 May and, as a way to enforce it, told embassies in Kyiv to evacuate and warned of a massive missile strike on the city centre. The fear of the attack was supposed to ensure Kyiv's compliance. This time nobody was scared. Kyiv ignored the threat completely, continuing to strike defence industry and oil infrastructure deep inside Russia. Russia issued threats it could not back up with actions and had to retreat. Ukraine did not even issue threats, but Russia had to seek protection from Donald Trump, and the only way Trump could deliver Ukrainian compliance was by meeting Kyiv's price — a prisoner exchange.
The humiliation this brought was unrivalled and very public. Putin now has to act — except that his options are very limited, each worse than the last.
Kneejerk Threats

The initial reaction was taken straight from the standard Kremlin rulebook. At a press conference following bilateral meetings in the Kremlin on 9 May, Putin tried to inflate Russian significance and present Russia as a major player in the South Caucasus by suggesting Armenia put its EU aspirations to a public vote via referendum — as if Russia had any say in Armenia's future. He also issued a thinly veiled threat by reminding his audience that the current situation in Ukraine was a consequence of Ukraine's EU bid.

This, of course, was the direct result of the Western leader summit (sans US) in Yerevan showing the Kremlin's diminishing reach in the region. In truth, the longer the war in Ukraine lasts, the further Armenia moves from Russia. Additionally, Armenian integration in the EU is in Azerbaijan's interests — the last thing Baku wants is for Russia to renew its destabilisation attempts in the region. Short of removing the Prime Minister of Armenia, Nikol Pashinyan, by force, there is not much Russia can do to change Armenia's course while Russia is otherwise occupied. Russia is slowly learning that its threats can no longer change outcomes.

In the same speech, Putin stated that he was amenable to meeting with Volodymyr Zelenskyy even outside Moscow, which is where he had previously insisted the meeting should happen. This was supposed to show his flexibility — however, he immediately walked that back by insisting that any such meeting was to be held only to sign the final agreement. Considering that his presidential aide Yuri Ushakov had reaffirmed on 10 May that Moscow would only start negotiating in earnest once Ukraine had walked out of Donbas, Putin clearly meant the meeting was intended to accept Zelenskyy's capitulation. Russia's position has not changed one bit — they still want control over as much of Ukraine as they can get, even if they cannot take it by force.

And finally, Putin tried to walk back on the prisoner exchange — the price Russia was supposed to pay for the humiliation on Red Square. President Zelenskyy did not even deign to grant Putin any agency in this matter — he announced that the US was in charge of the prisoner swap. Putin could try to weasel out of it, but he would be punishing the US as the guarantor of the agreement, not Ukraine as the beneficiary.

All the traditional Kremlin tactics have stopped working. And yet a response is required. Putin must prove to his elites that he has not lost control, grip, and influence over the country and the situation.
Three Doors, No Exit
At this point, Putin has three choices available to him.

He can push forward with military action and try to take the remainder of Donbas to show he has not been throwing around empty threats. In current circumstances this would be almost impossible to achieve — with the Russian offensive coming to a virtual standstill, Russia might soon be walking backwards instead of forwards. Ukraine's mid-range drones — known among Ukrainian engineers as "middle-strikes" — have extended the kill zone, a term popularised by former commander-in-chief Zaluzhny to describe territory controlled by enemy drones where all movement stops, to up to 250km behind Russian lines, effectively collapsing logistics and supply chains.

Ukraine's plan to kill more troops than Russia can recruit is slowly showing results. With current recruitment levels Russia cannot sustain traditional offensives. More manpower is needed, but the number of men willing to die for Putin's goals is dwindling. A new mobilisation is needed, and yet Putin does not dare call one. The grumbling in Russia has increased. Russians stayed largely uninvolved when Ukraine was invaded in their name, remained aloof when some of their compatriots were scooped off the streets and sent to the frontline meat-grinder, or when their opposition leader was murdered in prison. However, taking away their mobile internet connection turned out to be the rallying cause. And then, of course, there are the regions hosting oil processing plants which suddenly discovered that when you attack your neighbour, that neighbour can hit back. In other words, there is a palpable air of displeasure in Russia. It is not a sign of impending revolution — Russian people have abdicated any agency long ago. However, it is enough for Putin to fear the consequences of wider mobilisation, including individual non-compliance from the conscripts who might not want to die. And that leads to loss of control.

Putin can also stop yanking everybody's chain and give diplomacy a real chance. This, however, leaves him vulnerable to a different kind of reality — the one where millions of men who are used to killing, raping, and pillaging, plus getting paid handsomely for it, are released into wider society alongside former criminals recruited from prisons, and expected to come to terms with the fact that all that blood and violence has effectively bought them barely 1% of Ukraine's territory per year.

Between the promises of stability Putin gave his subjects and the chaos a demobilisation would unleash, the wild 90s, when criminal gangs created by chaos and violent elements returning from colonial wars in Afghanistan and Chechnya controlled Russian streets, will seem like a fond childhood memory.

Even the last argument — nuclear weapons — is a card that can never be played. Once used, you can no longer control the reaction of your adversaries, and dropping a nuclear bomb on Ukraine would not give Putin control over it. Putin is a coward (literally, according to the Russian opposition) and would never choose an option that can cost him everything and still not create a favourable outcome.
And this leaves Putin with door No 3. He can do nothing. This, however, will not mean that things will stay the same. Quite the opposite — they will only get worse.

The state of the Russian economy is such that according to almost all economists, including Russian ones in exile, even a miracle cannot save it. It is going down, and one day it will hit rock bottom. Time is the only variable. And it is capable of inflicting untold pain in the process even before it expires completely.

The other element is the stagnating (indeed, reversing) frontline. In April 2026, for the first time since Ukraine's Kursk incursion, Russia suffered a net loss of controlled territory. A year ago, it was gaining an average of 171 square miles a month. The loss of internet connection and overall quality of life were supposed to be offset by the glory of wiping out yet another village in Ukraine nobody in Russia had ever heard of before. Living much worse and not getting the glory people were promised can create the kind of cognitive dissonance that snowballs.

And then, of course, there is the ever-growing impact of Ukrainian attacks on Russian industrial targets. Most of the oil industry and defence sector has been built within city limits. When oil starts to rain from the sky or burn through streets to the accompaniment of air raid sirens, and the population increasingly has to search for shelters that do not exist, doing nothing no longer looks like a winning strategy.
The Cornered Rat

Putin has no good moves left. Every single action he takes brings him closer to an outcome he is trying to avoid. And so does inaction. In chess terms this is called zugzwang — a position where the obligation to move is itself the problem, because every available move makes things worse.

As a very gifted tactician and abysmal strategist, Putin can nonetheless employ moves that would temporarily correct the course of action. He did so in the autumn of 2022 when Russian defeat seemed unavoidable. The mobilisation and deployment of Iranian drones stalled the Ukrainian counter-offensive and stretched the war by several years. None of it changed the overall outcome — Russia will not be able to control Ukraine. What it did was drag other countries into the agony of the eventual collapse of Putin's power.

In his fight for relevance, Putin can still inflict untold suffering not only on Ukraine but on other neighbouring countries. After all, he is not constrained by the welfare of his subjects or even by sustaining minimum life-preserving conditions. For Putin, the goal is his own physical survival and that of his immediate family. The loss of power threatens that survival, so holding on to power is all that matters. Yet all the options available to him lead to loss of power, making him the proverbial cornered rat. This cornered rat has no plan but still has plenty of moves it can throw at us without worrying about consequences.

title: Russia Needs Ukraine's Permission to Hold a Military Parade
excerpt: Putin is so afraid of Ukrainian drones over Red Square that he ran to Trump. Why the 9 May parade matters so much, and what it no longer symbolises.

heroImage.alt: Russia Needs Ukraine's Permission to Hold a Military Parade

children.text: Donald Trump's and Vladimir Putin's last week's phone call last reignited discussions about a possible ceasefire in Russia's war against Ukraine. Could a ceasefire finally happen? Is there a light at the end of the tunnel? The answer to this was an obvious no and no. This was never about peace or even a ceasefire.

Putin is so terrified that his military parade on 9 May will be rained on by Ukrainian drones and possibly even missiles that he ran to Trump, complaining about Ukraine, and manoeuvred him into fronting a ceasefire request on the parade day. And then according to Zelenskyy Trump went and forgot to discuss any ceasefire with Ukraine.

Having failed to secure Ukraine's compliance via Trump, Russia unilaterally declared ceasefire from 8–9 May, threatening Ukraine with hell, retribution, annihilation — the usual. The Russian Defence Ministry even threatened European embassies in Kyiv, telling them to evacuate before the 9th of May. Ukraine answered by unilaterally declaring a regime of silence starting at midnight on the night of 5–6 May, which Russia promptly ignored. Consequently, Ukraine can now disregard Russian ceasefire.
So why is the parade so important and what does it symbolise?
A Parade in Want of Protection

The Kremlin's fear of Ukrainian disruption on the 9th of May festivities is hardly irrational. Ukraine has recently increased its attacks on Russian oil processing and export hubs. Additionally, at the end of February, Ukraine hit a missile assembly factory in Votkinsk some 1,400km from Ukraine using its FP-5 Flamingo cruise missile. It can hit Moscow – even if all missiles were shot down, the debris alone would wreak havoc for days. Even if Ukraine does not attack Moscow on the day of the parade, Moscow knows it has the means to do it; the fear and uncertainty alone are enough to set the Kremlin's nerves on edge.

Most of Russia's available air defence has already been redeployed around Moscow, leaving many oil processing plants open to Ukrainian long-range sanctions — Ukraine's own term for strikes that achieve what international sanctions have not. However, no air defence can guarantee complete safety.
As a result, Russia must either cancel its parade or secure Ukraine's agreement to the parade via other means.

Russia has already downsized the parade considerably. All military equipment has been excluded, although the planes might still be on the agenda. Cadets and other military students will be absent this year as well.

We should remember that the last few military parades held in Moscow have been equally thin and vulnerable. The downsizing started in 2022, when the flyover was cancelled at the last moment. Bad weather was cited. It was sunny that day in Moscow. At the time, Ukraine had no weapons that could reach Moscow. So why did they cancel the flyover? They feared their own. The short military operation Putin had planned did not go as expected. People were still allowed to grumble a bit. And the first reports of serious casualties were coming in. The leadership was seriously scared its own military could take revenge.

This fear materialised a year later in Prigozhin's march to Moscow on 24 June 2023. When the Kremlin dropped Prigozhin's plane two months later, the window of opportunity for any violent revolt closed. Everybody who had reasons and means to harm the leadership was at the front. And fear of their return is one of the reasons Putin is not finishing the war. Having allowed convicted murderers and rapists into the army to bolster recruitment figures and then mistreated them and allowed them to mistreat each other, the Kremlin lost any claim on their future allegiance.

Since then, the equipment portion of the parade has dwindled every year in line with meagre reserves, and most years saw no foreign dignitaries. Except for the parade of 2025, which marked 80 years since the end of WWII, and was attended by Xi Jinping, Aleksandar Vučić, and Nicolas Maduro, among others. Xi's presence in particular ensured that Ukraine would not gate-crash — open warfare with China has never been in Ukraine's interests.

This year there are no live hostages for Putin to hide behind. Although Slovak PM Fico attempted to arrange safe passage to Moscow, the Baltic states declared: he shall not pass. Reports suggest Fico intends to travel to Moscow via Belarus by train to lay flowers at the Tomb of the Unknown Soldier and meet with Putin, though he has said he will not participate in the parade.
The Myth, the Lies, and the Long Con

Most countries have a special day they celebrate — independence day. The three Baltic states even have two of them — the day of independence and the day independence was restored following the end of the Russian occupation. Russia does not have an independence day — it has no one to be independent from. Many countries do celebrate their independence from Russia, however.

During Soviet times, there were two days that were treated as sacred — the 9th of May, or Victory Day, and the 7th of November, which celebrated the communist coup of 1917. After the fall of the USSR, the communist coup ceased to be a happy memory, and only the 9th of May remained.

The reason Russia has always celebrated the end of WWII on the 9th of May, rather than the 8th like the rest of the world, has to do with Joseph Stalin. When the first capitulation of Nazi Germany was signed on the 7th of May 1945 in Reims, the representative of the Soviet Union signed it without having full authorisation from the 'dear leader'. The documents were signed for the second time on the 8th of May in Berlin. However, by the time the signing ceremony was finished, it was already past midnight in Moscow. Additionally, Stalin wanted to stress the importance of the USSR, so the Soviets uncoupled their Victory Day from everybody else's.

There was also another reason. While the rest of the world commemorated a war that lasted six years, the war the Soviets fought lasted only four — from 1941 to 1945. During the first two years of the war, from 1939 to 1941, Soviet Russia was allied with Hitler. In 1941, just over a month before the invasion of the USSR, Nazi officers were honoured guests at the May Day parade on Red Square.

It is worth noting that WWII started in September 1939, when Germany invaded Poland from the West and the Soviets invaded from the East. The USSR's war was different from everyone else's, and therefore so was its Victory Day. Additionally, while for the rest of Europe WWII ended in 1945, for Eastern European countries occupied by Soviet Russia it only ended nearly half a century later.
A Trump in the Pocket

Although the current parade has already been cut to the bone, it is still supposed to be a symbol of Russia's reach and ability to 'crush enemies' (or as it is - neighbours). Being throttled on this day in front of all and sundry would put a serious damper on the narrative of almighty Russia winning its war against tiny Ukraine that the locals are supposed to follow.

Additionally, the image of the strongman that Putin presents to his subjects depends on the visibility of that strength. Should anything unexpected happen on the 9th of May, that will expose the myth that Putin has built around him — the Russian ruler who 'raised Russia from its knees'. If Russia cannot protect Moscow on its most important day, then what did the million or so dead soldiers die for in Ukraine?

All in all, the fact that Putin is scrambling to find anybody capable of reining in Ukraine is rather ironic when we consider that four years ago on the 9th of May in 2022, Ukraine was seriously expecting to be hit by a nuclear missile in retaliation for having defeated Russia under Kyiv.

It is quite remarkable how the situation has turned around. Last year, Zelenskyy trolled the Kremlin, announcing that Ukraine could not ensure the security of the Victory Day parade in Moscow. This year, Ukraine's capability alone is enough to send Putin whining to Trump and even Europe as a last resort.
While Ukraine, having countered the Kremlin's bogus ceasefire with its own, now has its hands untied and ready to either strike Moscow at will or simply troll it mercilessly and watch it squirm.

title: The Parting of the Ways Across the Arab World
excerpt: The war in Iran has claimed its first institutional victim: OPEC. The UAE's exit is only the first step in a realignment across the Arab world.

heroImage.alt: The Parting of the Ways Across the Arab World

children.text: The war in Iran has just claimed its first victim: OPEC. The UAE's exit on Saturday leaves Saudi Arabia as the only member able to move prices by raising or lowering output. The producers who actually pump the most oil (the US, Canada, Brazil, Guyana) were never inside the cartel. With the Strait of Hormuz effectively closed, most Gulf members cannot get their oil out anyway, and the UAE has an alternative pipeline that bypasses the Strait — options other OPEC members do not have. OPEC is done.
Institutional failure

If leaving OPEC was not enough, Anwar Gargash, the UAE's long-time presidential diplomatic adviser, had signalled that the UAE would scrutinise its regional and international relationships. On Monday he named the GCC and the Arab League directly, calling their response to the attack on the UAE specifically "the weakest position in history".

Although an anonymous UAE official then told Reuters on Wednesday that any further withdrawals are not yet planned, the direction in which the UAE has been moving indicates that this is the beginning of a parting of the ways in the Persian Gulf and across the Arab world.

The UAE's reaction is not based simply on hurt feelings. It has been the country Iran attacked most heavily this time. Over the course of the war, Iran fired some 550 ballistic and cruise missiles and more than 2,200 drones at the UAE, more than at any other country, including Israel. Yet any attempts the UAE made to muster a united Arab front in response to Iran's indiscriminate attacks were rejected. In fact, on 8 April, hours after a US-brokered ceasefire was supposed to take effect, an Iranian refinery on Lavan Island was struck — Iranian sources and open-source analysts attributed the attack to UAE Mirage 2000 jets. On the same day, the Saudis were co-sponsoring a ceasefire framework.
Disagreement in the Making

However, the misalignment between the UAE and the other Gulf and Arab states was not caused by the war in Iran. The disagreements run deeper than that, and the war has only brought to a head a conflict that had been building for a while.

When Saudi Arabia signed a defence agreement with Pakistan in 2025, the UAE was unhappy – the UAE was deepening its ties with India, which is in an almost permanent state of conflict with Pakistan. On top of that, Pakistan's economy is structurally dependent on China, and the majority of its military technology is Chinese-made.

At the start of 2026, the UAE refused to extend its $3.45 billion loan facility, despite Pakistan asking for a two-year extension at a reduced interest rate. The loan was repaid in tranches in April, with the final payment on 23 April. Just two weeks earlier, on 11 April, Pakistan had deployed JF-17 fighter jets and military personnel to King Abdulaziz Air Base in Saudi Arabia under the Strategic Mutual Defence Agreement signed between the two countries in September 2025. Saudi Arabia replaced the UAE facility with $3 billion in fresh deposits to Pakistan's central bank and a year-long extension on $5 billion of existing loans.

So when Pakistan took the middle ground in the conflict instead of condemning the Iranian attacks on the UAE and other Gulf states, this was thought to have caused the Emirati ire. In fact, the UAE had been disengaging from Pakistan well before the US-Iran war began.
An Unlikely Ally

During the same period, the UAE was deepening ties with an unlikely regional ally — Israel. The UAE was one of the first countries to sign the Abraham Accords with Israel in 2020. This quickly led to a free trade agreement, intelligence and defence-industrial cooperation, and joint military operations.

In the early weeks of the war, as Iran was firing missiles and drones at the UAE in unprecedented numbers, Israel sent an Iron Dome battery, along with a contingent of IDF personnel to operate it, following a call between Netanyahu and Emirati President Mohammed bin Zayed. This was even as Israel itself was under attack from Iran. It marked the first time an Iron Dome battery was deployed outside Israel and the US.

In an article for the Arab Gulf States Institute, Tareq al-Otaiba, a former official at the UAE's National Security Council, wrote that the United States and Israel had "proved to be true allies by offering support through extensive military aid, intelligence sharing, and diplomatic backing." A senior Emirati official quoted by Axios was blunter: it was "a real eye-opening moment, to see who our real friends are." That list, the official said, also included France, the UK, Italy, and Australia. No GCC member appeared on it.
Going West

The differences go beyond military alliances. The UAE has been at odds with Qatar for over a decade over Doha's hosting of the Muslim Brotherhood, which the UAE designates as a terror group. That dispute now extends to the UK, where the Brotherhood remains perfectly legal and Qatari money is a major source of funding for British universities. The UAE has recently banned its students from attending UK universities — Emirati students, it seems, were coming home radicalised, and the UAE was unhappy about it.
The UAE is no longer prepared to wait for either Arab or Western consensus to catch up to its own position on political Islam.

While the Saudis aligned with Pakistan and by extension with China, the UAE aligned with Israel, the US, and the wider West. And leaving OPEC is only the first step in what looks to be the parting of the ways across the Arab world.

title: The Real Message Behind Ukraine's Offer to Negotiate
excerpt: Ukraine keeps inviting Russia to talk. The message is not that it expects Russia to come to its senses, and it is not addressed to whom you might think.

heroImage.alt: The Real Message Behind Ukraine's Offer to Negotiate

children.text: When Donald Trump returned to the White House, he promised to end the war in Ukraine by negotiating a deal between the warring parties, applying pressure on the party resisting the peace effort. We will never find out what would have happened had Washington actually tried to apply any pressure on anybody other than Ukraine. After Secretary of State Marco Rubio announced that the peace process had stalled and Washington was done waiting for results, many parties exhaled in relief. Not because they did not want peace but because the path chosen by Washington was never going to lead to peace.

Six days later, the EU, having mulled over the idea of appointing their own negotiator for talks with Russia, withdrew from the idea — and demonstrated how pointless they considered any negotiations with Russia by allowing EU High Representative for Foreign Affairs and Security Policy Kaja Kallas to revert to form. She declared that before any talks could begin, Russia would first have to agree to an unconditional ceasefire, withdraw its troops from Moldova and Georgia, reduce its armed forces, and cease its sabotage operations, cyber-attacks, and airspace violations across Europe. There was finally no need to pretend that anybody believed Russia in its current state could be reasoned with.

Then there was Zelenskyy's letter to Putin, in which he invited Putin to a face-to-face meeting (for an umpteenth time), called on him to end the war, and (the real reason the letter was sent) warned that Russian history does not favour rulers who do not know how to get out of sticky situations.
Why, if Trump no longer needs appeasing, is Ukraine still pretending that Russia can be reasoned with?
A Message Worth Looking Into

When Ukraine's officials issue a statement, it usually has a very specific purpose. We might not see that purpose for a while, but most of the time it becomes clear eventually. In this case, the important parts are the message and who it is addressed to.

The message Ukraine is sending is not that it expects Russia to come to its senses. It is that Ukraine intends to force Russia to see the reality in a new light. Looking at Ukraine's long-term plans that are finally coming to fruition, it is becoming clear that Kyiv is not talking of diplomacy at all. It is talking of negotiations where one side is being hammered from several different directions at once — directions it did not see coming — until giving up its goals in Ukraine no longer looks quite so unattractive.

Several events are converging at the moment. Following a personnel change at the start of the year, the new Minister of Defence Mykhailo Fedorov declared a new direction in the fight against Russia — Russian troop targets. If more men could be eliminated by Ukraine than can be recruited by Russia, Russian fighting capabilities would start shrinking.

In addition to this strategy, the middle-strikes programme finally came into its own earlier this year. The kill zone — the area around the front line reachable by drones — has extended from around 20 km to 150 km, and in some cases 200 km. Crucially, this reach applies to Ukrainian middle-strike drones, not Russian ones. Suddenly, Ukraine is capable of taking out equipment and manpower supply routes deep in the enemy's rear, while Russia does not have the same options.

This has compounded the loss of manpower and supplies to such an extent that Russia has virtually stopped advancing. According to the Ukrainian monitoring group DeepState, May 2026 marked the first month since 2023 in which Russia lost more territory than it gained; the Institute for the Study of War, using a different measure, dates the previous net loss to Ukraine's Kursk incursion in 2024. Either way, the direction is the same — a reversal that would have been unthinkable a year ago.

In addition to this, the campaign against the Russian energy industry is finally coming together. Having struck ports, fuel production facilities, and pumping stations, Ukraine has simultaneously forced Russia to reduce oil production — hitting the Russian budget — and driven it into a serious fuel crisis. There are fuel shortages across most of European Russia, and attacks on pumping stations are blocking deliveries from Western Siberia, while the insufficiently developed rail and road infrastructure provides no meaningful relief.

If that were not enough, the same fuel crisis in tandem with middle-strike drones is affecting Crimea especially hard. Following repeated attacks on the Kerch Bridge connecting mainland Russia with occupied Crimea, Russia stopped using it to transport fuel and ammunition — afraid of another strike turning any flammable cargo into the weapon itself. Ukraine then systematically picked off the ferries Russia had been using to supply the peninsula across the Kerch Strait instead, disabling the last of them in April 2026.

With supply by water now impossible, and rail routes through the occupied territories within reach of Ukrainian attack drones, Russia is left with an unwelcome choice: use the bridge to resupply the peninsula and risk exactly the kind of spectacular strike Ukraine has been waiting for, or leave Crimea without adequate supplies. If I were a betting woman, I would put a lot of money on Ukraine waiting patiently for a sufficiently explosive cargo to cross the bridge before finishing the job for good.

At the start of the full-scale invasion, the Kremlin touted the establishment of the land corridor to Crimea via the newly occupied territories as a palpable result of the invasion, sold to their own population as success. This land corridor is so vulnerable now that it is effectively gone, turning Crimea from an asset into a sitting duck.

These are the negotiations that Ukraine is conducting at the moment. And it seems that they expect the first realisation of the impending disaster to hit the Russian side just before winter. Hence the assessment of possible end of hostilities by winter.
For Whom the Bell Tolls
This was the answer to the question of what message the Ukrainian side is sending. Now, who is the message intended for?

It cannot be intended for Europe — Europe knows the score all too well without any outside direction. The message can therefore be meant for the US or Russia. While the US would appreciate the chance to take ownership of at least one war properly finished under its ministrations, by the time the measures instituted by Ukraine finally come to a head, the midterm elections will be over and Trump will most likely have lost the Republican majority in Congress.

Which is why this message is most likely intended for Russia. Even Zelenskyy's letter to Putin. Not for the population, which has no mechanism and no history of acting, but for the small circle around Putin who can. The ones who risk losing their fortunes, lifestyles and access to power, and who will eventually have to calculate whether hastening his demise is less dangerous than riding the war down with him. There is no way to know how deep their pain threshold runs. It would seem that Ukraine expects them to get the first hint by the end of this year. How many hints it will take before the message finally sinks in, is anybody's question.

title: Two Nuclear Panics, One Playbook
excerpt: Two nuclear scares three years apart, spread by two very different men, following exactly the same script. The parallels are striking.

children.text: When on 7 April Donald Trump delivered his now infamous tweet (and I know, by now it's a tall order classifying which one is more infamous than others) on an entire civilisation dying that night, some took it as incoherent ramblings of a feeble mind, and others as 'art of the deal'. There were some sardonic responses, not least from Larry the Cat enquiring on Twitter if Trump had specified which civilisation he was talking about.

By now, most people had got used to treating everything Donald Trump says with a pinch of salt – he tends to attack, threaten, and when the threat does not reach the target – retreat. The few times he has meted out any violence, it was after posting reconciliatory messages. If anything, those are more dangerous.

Which is why it was a bit odd that Tucker Carlson proceeded to conclude that Trump's missive might indicate imminent deployment of nuclear weapons and urged the US military not to comply with such orders. Panic ensued.

On its own, this looks simply like an odd episode. However, what people not living in several information bubbles at the same time might not know or remember is that almost exactly the same episode of panic happened in May 2023, when another blogger similarly became the source of panic after spreading information on the imminent nuclear attack Russia was planning that night against Ukraine. It was almost identical, including the pleas to the Russian military not to comply with criminal orders.

In the case of 2023, the panic was spread by a Russian blogger in exile, Vladimir Osechkin, the founder of Gulagu.net, a human rights organisation documenting abuses in Russia's penal system. He became known to a wider audience after publishing leaked videos of inhuman torture of inmates in Russian penal colonies. One of his sources was a supposed whistleblower from the FSB, 'The Wind of Change', who fed him long missives on the mental deterioration in the midst of the Russian intelligence cohort — with serious arguments made both for and against this being an FSB disinformation operation in its own right. It was therefore unsurprising that Osechkin made for an ideal conduit for a textbook Russian disinformation operation. He was led to believe that Russia was planning to hit Ukraine with nuclear weapons that night, in response to Ukraine targeting the Russian flag atop the Kremlin the day before with a drone.

Osechkin livestreamed his panic and pleaded with the Russian military in charge of the nuclear arsenal not to follow what would be criminal orders. No nuclear bombing followed, and Osechkin ended up being the butt of jokes within the Russian opposition segment for several months.

Whether or not Carlson created essentially the same effect in the US knowingly or not, the parallels are striking. Carlson's interview with Putin (or to be more precise – Putin's historical lecture given to Carlson on the origins of Russian tradition), as well as later interviews with Dugin, the ideologist most closely associated with the intellectual underpinnings of Russia's imperial project, and foreign minister Lavrov, as well as his documented admiration of Russian supermarkets, all indicate that he can at the very least in theory be seen as a conduit of messages produced by Russian information ops. The man has been defending Russia's right to attack Ukraine for years. And for all we know, he might actually believe it. Or perhaps such views are a matter of choice. In any event, his intellectual abilities are difficult to ascertain.

As we can see, both men caused panic about a nuclear power purportedly getting ready to use nuclear weapons on a non-nuclear country. In both cases the claims were ludicrous and baseless. Citing Trump's words as cause for concern by now is childish – the man has issued every single type of tweet known to mankind, all contradicting each other. There is simply no scenario in which the US would use nuclear weapons on a country for not submitting to the will of Don. And while it is more difficult to claim the same in the Russian case, we are very far from a scenario in which it would make any sense even to a mass murderer like Putin.
Or it might just be a coincidence. Right? Right!?
Oh, and why?

title: Washington and 5000 Virtual Troops
excerpt: Troops withdrawn, troops returned, nothing changed. What the virtual movement of 5,000 American soldiers says about Washington's real agenda in Europe.

children.text: On Thursday, Donald Trump announced on Truth Social that due to Poland having elected a president he liked (although that happened over a year ago, but Trump is almost 80, so let's not be too harsh) 5,000 American troops would be deployed to Poland as a contribution to the security of NATO's eastern flank. This seemingly contradicted earlier plans announced by the Pentagon not to return 5,000 troops to Poland after their rotation.

The timing around the confusion about the lost or returned American troops however was telling — it happened just on the eve of the meeting of NATO foreign ministers in Helsingborg, Sweden. The last time a meeting like this happened was December 2025, and back then Secretary of State Marco Rubio was conspicuously absent — he sent a deputy. This time Rubio was present. It would seem Washington had an agenda, and placating Europeans with an additional troop deployment was necessary to advance it.

What was missing in December was not just American interest in NATO, but also war in the Middle East. In December, Washington did not yet need anything from Europe. Having got stuck in Iran, Washington has been trying to engage Europe in unblocking the Strait of Hormuz to no avail. Rubio was dispatched to try to change their minds.

Withdrawing troops from Europe as a punishment and then sending them there again fits Washington's transactional approach to 'close allied relationships'. Earlier this month, Trump, having taken umbrage at German Chancellor Friedrich Merz, declared the reduction of American troops in Germany by 5,000 men. Around the same time, a further deployment of several hundred troops was cancelled along with the long-awaited deployment of long-range missiles to Germany.

With this in mind, the newly declared deployment of 5,000 troops to Poland might seem like a positive development — until we factor in Polish Foreign Minister Radosław Sikorski's statement that after the new deployment Poland will host roughly the same number of US troops as before. In other words, nothing really changed.
So what was this virtual troop movement all about?

Officially the US was to discuss an increase in defence spending, unblocking the Strait of Hormuz, and the use of European air bases for American operations in the Middle East. Even before the meeting, other NATO foreign ministers stated that their countries were going to get involved in securing the Strait of Hormuz… once conditions allowed.

Rubio was obviously shipped off to Helsingborg to try to strongarm Europe into helping the US in the Middle East. While the earlier decision on the troop withdrawal from Germany looks like a bog-standard display of Trump's wrath at Europe, and the cancellation of the missile deployment might be the result of the US war in Iran (the Pentagon may need them elsewhere), the refusal to rotate troops in Poland and then their miraculous deployment seems like a carrot and stick intended, if not exactly to bring Europe to heel (Washington is beginning to realise that pushing Europe around does not elicit the right response) then at least to get it much closer to the US position.

While the withdrawal of troops can hurt Europe to a certain extent, it is not the number that matters — it is the show of support from Washington, and sadly it is not forthcoming. The White House seems hell-bent on demonstrating its indifference to all things European. In this light, the deployment of Tomahawks to Germany was going to be the next best thing — if Europe cannot rely on Washington, it can at least rely on the hardware to hit choice targets in Russia if push came to shove. Even that option is now under threat.

With both political and military support by the US withdrawn from Europe, Rubio's trip to Sweden seems very naïve. Washington was genuinely hoping to blackmail Europe into helping the US with Iran. It expects Europe not to notice that US support vis-à-vis Russia has been withdrawn, and any promises can be cancelled by a single post on Truth Social. Yet Washington seems to believe that it can talk or force Europe into engaging with the Middle Eastern crisis while at the same time hoping to hoodwink Europe on security. Or maybe Washington has taken a leaf out of Russia's book and thought to offer thin air in exchange for European actions.

Although Europe is still balancing between outright opposition to Washington and the need for defensive technologies, it is no longer playing the sycophantic game with Trump. It knows full well Trump's America is not an ally, and it will not get involved in Trump's problems, since Trump is not interested in Europe's problems. And dragging a few thousand US troops around Europe virtually or physically is not going to change that.

title: Will Iran Get Its Bucket List?
excerpt: The US and Iran are about to sign an agreement on an agreement. Iran's wishlist is almost complete, while US interests are notably absent.

heroImage.alt: Will Iran Get Its Bucket List?

children.text: The deal between the US and Iran, we are told, is all but agreed on and will be signed this Friday. Will the US and Iran reach an agreement, how will that affect the rest of us, and what does the situation tell us so far?
There is very little information available on the possible terms of the agreement, and that in itself is very telling.

First, the document that is rumoured to be signed is a Memorandum of Understanding. Effectively, it is an agreement on the agreement to reach an agreement. Basically, they agreed to talk. And yet the terms of what they will discuss are already in play.

So far, the terms of the MoU have been leaked mostly by the Iranian side. Bloomberg claims to have seen a version of the document from somebody on the US side, while Reuters has reported on several conditions potentially presented by an Iranian source. What all of these versions have in common is that Iran's wishlist is almost complete, while US interests are notably absent.
The Iranian Wishlist Galore

From what we understand based on the collective terms of all these leaks taken together, Iran will have oil sanctions lifted, allowing it to sell oil again, it will get its assets released, naval blockade lifted, ceasefire on all fronts, including Lebanon. Iran also gets to keep its ballistic programme. A full US military withdrawal from the Persian Gulf region is also on the list, as is the reconstruction programme worth minimum $300 billion, funded by US and "regional partners". It might or might not be contingent on further talks, but these are effectively reparations. From the US. To Iran.

For that, Iran will consent to open the Strait of Hormuz, supposedly free of charge but under the control of Iran (and Oman) nevertheless. And it will pinky promise not to pursue nuclear weapons for an x number of years, although it will still continue enriching uranium. But it will allow in IAEA inspectors… because it worked the last time.

What these leaks and, in fact, the actual lack of information tells us is that the terms agreed are nothing to be proud of, at least when it comes to the US. While Iran seems to be happy to publicise them, the achievement the US is touting is the signing of the MoU, not the terms under which it will be (if at all) signed. That bad, eh?

This actually indicates that the deal might be struck. Since the goals of both parties are completely opposite — Iran needs its nuclear programme to maintain regional hegemony, influence over its terror proxies and continue its fight against Israel, while the United States wants to make sure Iran cannot do it — in order for the agreement to be reached, one side has to give up its goals. If even half of these terms are accurate, Iran seems to be getting a complete bucket list.
We might hope that not all of Iran's wishes will be granted, however, the silence from Washington and Trump's desperation for a deal might work in Iran's favour here.
The Dues that Will Be Due

What does it mean for the rest of us? The ones who do not get the vote but still have to live with the consequences of the choice the US voters make. As usual, there are good news and bad news. Let's start with the good news.

The Strait of Hormuz should be open at least for a while. Eventually, the Middle East will learn to diversify its shipping routes and the oil, fuel, and liquefied gas will reach the consumers without going through the Gulf. That might gradually reduce the influence Iran has achieved as a result of this war. However, that might take a while.

Now to the bad news. If the agreement is signed and if it is then kept, the US will have lost its influence in the Middle East. From now on, the power in the region will belong to Iran and its allies. Washington might think it still has a say, but after it presided over a debacle that resulted in Dubai losing the status of a desirable destination for the rich and powerful, cut down the Gulf state income and left Middle Eastern countries to fend for their own defence, Trump will not be getting any more airplanes or golden trinkets as bribes.

Iran will continue work on its ballistic programme. Considering that the current one posed a danger to the entire Middle East and painted a target over Europe, we are most likely not going to enjoy the new and improved one either.
As to the nuclear programme… Yeah, of course they will not pursue it. Why should they?

Support for Islamic terrorist organisations like Hezbollah, Hamas, and Houthis will remain. As will their collective fight against Israel, who will inevitably retaliate. Looking weak in the Middle East is tantamount to putting a bounty on one's own head. Not a good idea for either of the sides.

On a larger scale, if Russia's aggression against Ukraine has not taught the world the importance of having nuclear weapons, then the demise of the US hegemony in the region and Iran's new-found influence should do the trick.
Same Problems, Larger Scale

Based on what we think we know, Iran has ample reasons to sign the deal. Even if the terms of the agreement are less favourable to Iran than reported, as long as it dangles the final agreement in front of Washington, it can extract concessions from the US and does not have to give up anything it is not willing to part with.

Whether or not an agreement is reached (and then adhered to), the threat Iran posed before the United States attacked it in February has not been removed. If anything, Iran is now stronger than ever, while Washington's influence in the region is reduced to a bare minimum, leaving its one-time allies vulnerable. Iran has shown that a smaller actor can terrorise an entire region, get away with it and walk away with the prize. The oil might start flowing again, but the increased fuel supply and reduced prices might end up being a poor substitute for global stability.
